_comment: Infrastructure providers behind tracked neobanks. clients[] must use exact entity names from data.json. Relationships come from issuer disclosures on the neobanks' own sites/cards or widely reported public sources; some are historical (noted in about).

The Bancorp Bank.about: The original fintech sponsor bank — quietly issues cards and holds deposits for a huge share of US consumer fintech, Chime included (alongside Stride).

Stride Bank.about: Oklahoma national bank that co-sponsors Chime's card programs with Bancorp — proof that a community bank charter can sit under tens of millions of fintech accounts.

Evolve Bank & Trust.about: One of the busiest BaaS sponsor banks of the boom era; its 2024 Synapse fallout and enforcement action made it the cautionary tale of the model. Still sits under Dave and Step.

Choice Financial Group.about: North Dakota sponsor bank behind some of the biggest US fintech brands — a long-time partner bank for both Current and Mercury deposits.

Cross River Bank.about: New Jersey's fintech-lending powerhouse — issues Current's credit builder card and, since 2026, the US MetaMask Card, putting a state-chartered bank under the world's default self-custody wallet.

Coastal Community Bank.about: Washington-state charter that powers One (Walmart's fintech) among a large CCBX partner portfolio — one of the few sponsor banks that grew through the 2023-24 BaaS crackdown.

Column.about: The developer-first nationally chartered bank built by Plaid co-founder William Hockey — no middleware, direct Fed access. Banks Mercury and Flex among fintech's most technical teams.

Lead Bank.about: Jackie Reses' (ex-Square Capital) Kansas City charter, rebuilt as a fintech sponsor bank — issues Flex's Visa Infinite business credit card.

Thread Bank.about: Tennessee charter behind embedded deposit programs with $3M pass-through FDIC sweep networks — sits under Relay and part of Flex's banking stack.

Sutton Bank.about: Ohio community bank that issues the Cash App Card — a ~$1B-asset bank under one of America's biggest consumer money apps.

WebBank.about: Utah industrial bank that issues the Klarna Card in the US — the charter of choice for point-of-sale credit programs.

Solaris.about: Europe's flagship banking-as-a-service platform with a full German banking licence; Tomorrow runs on it, and Vivid launched on it before getting its own EMI — BaFin pressure has since forced a hard pivot.

ClearBank.about: The UK's first new clearing bank in 250 years — holds Tide's customer funds and provides agency banking rails to dozens of UK fintechs.

Marqeta.about: The public (NASDAQ: MQ) modern card-issuing platform that grew up processing Cash App's card volume; also powers Klarna's card programs and newer entrants like BFinance.

Galileo.about: The processor under the first US neobank wave — Chime (before its in-house ChimeCore migration), Dave and MoneyLion all built on it. Acquired by SoFi in 2020 for $1.2B.

Baanx (Monavate).about: The card-program layer between self-custody wallets and Mastercard/Visa rails — powers the MetaMask Card and 1inch Card; merged with Monavate in 2026.

Rain.about: Visa-principal-member issuing platform for stablecoin-native cards — programs settle in USDC onchain. Issues Karta among a fast-growing roster of web3 card programs.

Immersve.about: Mastercard-partnered protocol that lets wallets spend directly from self-custody — the issuing layer behind Bitget Wallet's card.

Unblock (Atomo).about: Swiss VQF-regulated Atomo SARL is a Mastercard principal member offering white-label card programs settled in fiat or stablecoins — issues Mine's card across 175+ countries.

Wirex Pay.about: Wirex's infrastructure arm — a decade of crypto-card issuing experience packaged as white-label rails, powering COCA's cards.

Bridge (Stripe).about: Stripe's $1.1B stablecoin-orchestration acquisition — issues branded stablecoins and moves money on stablecoin rails. Issues Deel's DLUSD and MetaMask's mUSD (with M0).

Privy (Stripe).about: Embedded-wallet layer (acquired by Stripe in 2025) that gives fintech users an onchain wallet behind a normal login — part of the stack under Deel's DLUSD and Karta.

Tempo.about: The payments-first L1 blockchain incubated by Stripe and Paradigm — purpose-built rails for stablecoin settlement, used in Deel's and Karta's stacks.

Monerium.about: Icelandic e-money institution issuing EURe — the regulated onchain euro with personal IBANs that Gnosis Pay accounts are built on.

M0.about: Shared stablecoin issuance protocol that lets brands mint their own fully-reserved dollar — powers MetaMask's mUSD (issued with Bridge), the first wallet-native stablecoin.

Paxos.about: NYDFS-regulated brokerage and tokenization platform that let mainstream apps switch on crypto — powered Nubank Cripto, Mercado Pago's crypto launch and Revolut's US debut.

Zero Hash.about: B2B crypto and stablecoin settlement layer with money-transmitter coverage across the US — the plumbing behind MoneyLion's and Juno's crypto features.

Fireblocks.about: MPC custody and transfer network securing digital-asset operations for thousands of institutions — Revolut among the fintechs running treasury and wallet ops on it.

Pomelo.about: LatAm's modern card-issuing platform, built because Marqeta never localized — issues for the region's crypto neobanks including Lemon and Belo.

Mambu.about: The SaaS core-banking pioneer: N26 famously launched on it before building in-house, and TymeBank runs Africa's biggest digital bank on it today.

Thought Machine.about: Google-engineer-founded Vault core, the ledger under Atom Bank since 2016 — Curve and Monese both moved onto it as they scaled.

Treezor.about: Société Générale-owned French BaaS platform — the original rails under Shine and much of the French fintech wave.

Synapse.about: The a16z-backed BaaS middleware whose 2024 bankruptcy froze ~$200M of end-user funds across partner banks — Juno was among the apps caught in the collapse. The reason 'who holds the ledger?' is now a due-diligence question.

Blend.about: Non-custodial yield layer for neobanks: each user gets their own Gnosis Safe with sanctions screening and audit trail built in, and capital routes across risk-rated DeFi venues (Morpho, Aave) — one SDK instead of a protocol-by-protocol integration.

NAYA.about: AI-powered financial-operations backbone — unified double-entry ledger, reconciliation engine and data hub sitting between a neobank's product and its banking partners, so balances and bank statements never drift apart.

Plaid.about: The account-connectivity network that links bank accounts to apps — Chime, Current, Varo and Dave all rely on it for funding flows and account linking.

Pathward.about: The former MetaBank, one of America's largest prepaid and BaaS charters — bank of record for MoneyLion's RoarMoney account, with the partnership extended to 2029.

Community Federal Savings Bank.about: The small Queens thrift under a startling share of global fintech's US rails — Wise's USD accounts, Crypto.com's prepaid card, Greenlight, Zolve and Comun all sit on it. A 2025 OCC order to rebuild its AML program showed how much weight the charter carries.

Metropolitan Commercial Bank.about: The New York bank that issued Crypto.com's first US Visa card (2018) and sat under Revolut US until 2024 — then exited crypto entirely in 2023 as regulators turned, handing its fintech book to successors like Lead Bank.

Lincoln Savings Bank.about: A 1902 Iowa community bank that quietly processes Cash App's direct deposits — and was MoneyLion's original deposit bank before Pathward. Proof the first US fintech wave ran on Main Street charters.

Sunrise Banks.about: CDFI-certified Minnesota charter that specializes in mission-driven fintech — issues MoCaFi's cards (including city disbursement programs like LA's Angeleno Card) and the True Link prepaid Visa for vulnerable adults.

Piermont Bank.about: A de novo New York charter (2019) built from scratch for embedded banking — issues Slash's business debit Mastercard alongside a curated fintech portfolio it keeps deliberately small.

Green Dot.about: The prepaid pioneer turned publicly listed BaaS bank (NYSE: GDOT) — its Arc platform runs Apple Cash and Walmart's money products, making it one of the biggest sponsor banks with no tracked neobank on it: its clients are mostly big tech and retail, not challengers.

Federal Bank.about: The Kerala bank that became Indian neobanking's default charter — Jupiter and Fi Money both built on its savings accounts. Fi's 2026 wind-down, with customers pushed to the bank's own FedMobile app, showed who really owns the relationship.

SBM Bank India.about: The Indian subsidiary of Mauritius' state bank, reborn as a fintech partner bank — powers Niyo Global's zero-forex savings account and debit card for travelling Indians.

Equitas Small Finance Bank.about: Chennai small-finance bank that hosted two tracked neobanks — Freo Save's digital savings account and Niyo's NiyoX (the Niyo tie-up ended May 2025, a reminder that Indian partner-bank deals are rented, not owned).

RBL Bank.about: One of India's most fintech-forward private banks — a core partner bank behind RazorpayX's business current accounts and corporate cards.

M2P Fintech.about: The API layer under India's card-fintech wave — Niyo, Jupiter and slice all built card programs on its bank-BIN orchestration. Now 30+ markets across MENA, ASEAN and Africa, backed by Visa and Helios.

Edenred Payment Solutions.about: Formerly PrePay Technologies/PPS, one of the UK's oldest e-money institutions (licensed 2003) — issued Monese's cards and accounts across 31 countries, the classic agent-of-EMI model that predates the BaaS label.

Swan.about: France's developer-darling embedded-banking EMI: French SaaS apps bolt a full 'compte pro' into their product — Indy's freelancer account runs on it, alongside Pennylane and much of French fintech's second wave.

Okali.about: The former SFPMEI, France's original BaaS e-money institution (now Crédit Agricole-owned) — carried Lydia from 2013 until it won its own EMI licence in 2023 and dropped 'OKALI' from customers' bank statements.

Enfuce.about: Finland's card-as-a-service heavyweight, founded by two ex-bankers in a sauna — took Sweden's Rocker from zero to 100,000+ cards in two years, handling licensing, processing and compliance in one stack.

Engine by Starling.about: Starling Bank's core, packaged as SaaS for other banks — powered Salt Bank's build-and-launch in under 12 months, with 100,000 Romanian customers onboarded in the first two weeks.

Modulr.about: UK e-money institution with direct Faster Payments and Bacs access — provides the payment accounts and scheme connectivity behind Pockit, and embedded payments for hundreds of UK platforms.

Griffin.about: The 'bank you can build on': a full UK banking licence (2024) wrapped in an API, founded by ex-Airbnb and CircleCI engineers — the first British BaaS provider that is actually a bank rather than an EMI. No tracked neobank runs on it yet.

Dock.about: LatAm's largest card-issuing and BaaS unicorn (ex-Conductor), processing for 70M+ active accounts — Mercado Pago, PagBank, C6 Bank and Neon have all run on its rails.

Pismo.about: Cloud-native core banking and payments platform acquired by Visa for $1B in 2024 — NG.cash migrated its 1M+ Gen-Z accounts onto it to cut costs and ship faster.

Union54.about: The YC-backed Zambian card-issuing API that served ~100 African fintechs — until a $1.2B attempted-fraud wave and Mastercard chargeback breaches forced a 2022 shutdown, cutting off virtual dollar cards at Eversend and dozens of peers overnight. Africa's Synapse-grade cautionary tale.

Providus Bank.about: Nigeria's go-to fintech partner bank — its virtual-NUBAN and BIN-sponsorship APIs sit under Piggyvest, Paystack and much of the Lagos fintech scene, though the tracked Nigerian neobanks (Kuda, OPay, PalmPay) hold their own licences.

NymCard.about: The only MENA BaaS provider fully regulated by the UAE central bank, and a Visa/Mastercard principal member — BIN sponsorship plus issuing across UAE, Saudi, Egypt and Pakistan, where it launched the country's first digital credit card with easypaisa.

RAKBANK.about: The 1976 Emirati bank that put the UAE's first independent neobank on the map — provided IBAN and BIN sponsorship for YAP's 2021 launch (YAP has since moved to Ruya Islamic Bank).

Thredd.about: The processor formerly known as GPS (Global Processing Services) — the issuer-processing firepower under the UK fintech wave: Revolut, Starling and Curve all scaled on it, and it still processes billions of transactions a year across 40+ countries.

i2c.about: Silicon Valley's quiet veteran (founded 2001) with a single global SaaS platform spanning 200+ countries — the processor behind the Crypto.com Visa card's worldwide rollout and Zolve's US cards for new arrivals.

Paymentology.about: The 2021 Paymentology–Tutuka merger created the first truly global issuer-processor, strongest in emerging markets — it processes cards for Tyme's digital banks in both South Africa and the Philippines.

Stripe Issuing.about: Stripe's card-issuing arm — the infrastructure under Ramp's 2M+ corporate cards since 2020, now pushing stablecoin-backed cards into markets its US clients couldn't previously reach.

Nium.about: The Singapore cross-border payments platform (ex-InstaReM) whose card-issuing APIs put Visa corporate cards inside Aspire, Southeast Asia's first business neobank.

Lithic.about: Started as consumer virtual-card app Privacy.com, then flipped its own infrastructure into a developer-first issuing API — the leanest way for US fintechs to spin up a card program.

Highnote.about: Built by the ex-Braintree/PayPal team as a from-scratch issuing platform with an embedded ledger — the newest US challenger to Marqeta and Lithic in embedded card programs.

Episode Six.about: Ledger-and-issuing platform built by former Visa executives whose pitch is currency-agnostic 'anything as money' — powers HSBC's PayMe wallet in Hong Kong among bank-grade clients.

CoreCard.about: The publicly listed (NYSE: CCRD) credit-card processing specialist that quietly runs the Apple Card for Goldman Sachs — proof that complex revolving-credit programs still need deep specialist cores.

Zeta.about: Bhavin Turakhia's $1.5B-valued banking-tech bet: the cloud-native Tachyon processing stack behind HDFC Bank's PayZapp and Pixel credit card, now pushing into US credit unions.

Wallester.about: Estonian Visa principal member offering white-label card issuing with a free-tier API — the fast, low-ceremony route to a European card program for early-stage fintechs.

Adyen Issuing.about: The acquiring giant's issuing arm — Adyen's EU banking licence and single-platform pitch let platforms add branded cards next to the payments they already process.

Temenos.about: The 30-year incumbent that 3,000+ banks run on — and quietly the core under a surprising share of neobanks: Varo's US charter, Italy's Flowe (live in 5 months), Alpian, Judo Bank, EQ Bank and Ualá's Mexican bank all sit on it.

10x Banking.about: Ex-Barclays CEO Antony Jenkins' cloud-native 'SuperCore', backed by BlackRock and J.P. Morgan — the ledger JPMorgan chose to power Chase UK's assault on the British market.

Technisys.about: The LatAm-born Cyberbank core SoFi bought for $1.1B in 2022 to own its own ledger — SoFi's checking, savings and credit card are migrating onto it, folded into Galileo as 'Cyberbank Core'.

Finastra.about: The Misys+D+H legacy giant whose cloud-first Essence core got a second life with digital banks — Tonik launched Southeast Asia's first pure-play licensed neobank on it in 2021.

Finxact.about: Frank Sanchez's cloud-native 'core-as-a-service', acquired by Fiserv in 2022 — the elastic system of record One (Walmart's fintech) picked to run save-spend-borrow in a single account.

Finacle.about: Infosys' universal banking suite serving banks in 100 countries — the incumbent core that emerging-market digital brands are often carved out of, including Emirates NBD's Liv., the UAE's first lifestyle neobank.

Tuum.about: Estonian API-first core built by ex-bank-IT veterans — migrated millions of LHV UK accounts with zero downtime, making it the credible European challenger to Mambu and Thought Machine.

Oradian.about: Cloud core built for emerging markets and microfinance-scale economics — FairMoney runs Nigeria's biggest credit-led neobank (12M+ accounts) on its Instafin platform.

SaaScada.about: Event-driven core-banking engine born inside a UK charity-banking project — sells real-time data as the product, one of the few cores designed around inclusion use cases first.

Circle.about: Issuer of USDC, the regulated digital dollar that a whole wave of neobanks treat as their core ledger — Mine settles IBAN deposits straight into it, COCA plugs into Circle's payment platform, and USDC-native apps from Daimo to Kontigo build on its rails.

Tether.about: Issuer of USDT, the world's most-held stablecoin — and increasingly a strategic investor in the apps that distribute it: Oobit and Fizen are Tether-backed, and Plasma One runs on the Tether-linked Plasma chain.

BVNK.about: Enterprise stablecoin payments stack ($30B+ annualized volume) behind embedded wallets and payouts for Deel and Bitso — Mastercard agreed to acquire it for up to $1.8B in 2026, the largest stablecoin-infra deal on record.

Perena.about: Solana-native stablecoin infrastructure (the USD* 'numeraire' pool) founded by Solana Foundation's ex-stablecoin lead — the yield engine behind SurfCash's up-to-15% rates.

Kulipa.about: Paris-based stablecoin card issuer that connects self-custody wallets to Visa/Mastercard via smart-contract session keys — never touching user funds. Powers the Ready (ex-Argent) and Solflare cards; 120,000+ cards issued since 2025.

Reap.about: Hong Kong Visa Principal Issuer (also Mexico) whose white-label card-as-a-service sits under RedotPay and KAST — one of only three partners Visa names in its 160+ stablecoin card programs. Acquired by Kraken's parent Payward in 2026.

Striga.about: Estonian MiCA-licensed API stack for EU crypto neobanks — one integration covers custody, trading, virtual IBANs and Visa/Mastercard issuing from bitcoin or stablecoin balances, the plug-and-play way to launch a European crypto card.

Turnkey.about: Key-management infrastructure from the team that built Coinbase Custody — secure-enclave signing behind 50M+ embedded wallets, the default answer when a consumer money app needs seedless self-custody at scale.

Dfns.about: Paris-based MPC wallet-as-a-service repositioned as a 'core banking platform for digital assets' — powers the self-custody wallet infrastructure behind Deblock, France's first MiCA-licensed institution.

Web3Auth.about: MPC key infrastructure that turns social logins into non-custodial wallets — powers Trust Wallet's seedless onboarding and was acquired by Consensys (MetaMask's parent) in 2025.

Safe.about: The smart-account standard securing $100B+ onchain — Gnosis Pay wires its Visa card directly to each user's Safe, and EtherFi Cash holds collateral in per-user Safes. The de facto account layer of onchain banking.

MoonPay.about: The consumer-fintech of crypto onramps — card and bank rails into 500+ apps across 190 countries, including the buy/sell flows inside MetaMask, Trust Wallet (multi-year exclusive since 2025) and Phantom.

Transak.about: Fiat-to-crypto ramp behind 450+ apps and MetaMask's long-time primary onramp — since September 2025 the exclusive provider of MetaMask's Deposit button, with 1:1 stablecoin purchases and named IBANs.

Ramp Network.about: Warsaw-built onramp/offramp where one KYC unlocks hundreds of wallets — powers buy and L2 cash-out flows in MetaMask and crypto purchases in Trust Wallet across 150+ countries.

Morpho.about: The DeFi lending network that neobanks embed as their savings backend — Deblock's 4% euro current account runs on a Steakhouse-curated Morpho vault, and Hyperbeat borrows via Morpho at the point of sale.

Aave.about: DeFi's largest lending protocol (~$50B TVL), increasingly consumed as embedded yield: MetaMask's Stablecoin Earn deposits straight into Aave and auto-routes swaps into aTokens, and Mine's Earn runs on Aave V3.

Linea.about: Consensys' Ethereum L2 and MetaMask's home network for money — every MetaMask Card transaction executes on Linea, and mUSD launched natively there.

Celo.about: Mobile-first chain (now an Ethereum L2) purpose-built for cheap stablecoin payments — the rails under Opera's MiniPay and its 16M+ wallets across the Global South.

Gnosis Chain.about: The low-cost EVM chain where Gnosis Pay settles — each cardholder's Safe account lives on Gnosis Chain, making it the first chain with a Visa card wired directly to it.

Stellar.about: The 2014-vintage payments-first blockchain with native USDC support — the settlement rails under COCA's self-banking accounts and a long roster of remittance corridors.

Plasma.about: Tether-linked L1 purpose-built for zero-fee USD₮ transfers — the chain that Plasma One, its own consumer neobank, runs on. Vertical integration of chain and money app in one stack.

Solana.about: The high-throughput chain that markets itself as payments infrastructure (Solana Pay) — Sling Money's stablecoin P2P, Decaf's spending rails, KAST's stablecoin cards and Solflare all run their money features on it.

Mercuryo.about: Payments infrastructure for wallets and exchanges — fiat on/off-ramps plus card-issuing rails (its Spend product powers wallet-linked cards across Europe).

OpenPayd.about: Embedded-finance and banking-as-a-service platform: virtual IBANs, multi-currency accounts and stablecoin ramps consumed via a single API.
